narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your outstanding undergraduate GPA of 3.90—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a solid LSAT score of 40, which exceeds the usual 36.70 mark, along with your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 43.9% chance you'll face failure on that crucial test, tipping the scales toward rejecting your application to prioritize those with lower predicted risks. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, far below the average 5.53, signaling to the model a concerning dip in later academic performance compared to peers. On a brighter note, your strong LSAT score was the most powerful force pushing toward a passing prediction, countering some doubts effectively. Your high undergraduate GPA also worked strongly in your favor, nudging the outlook positively much like it does for top performers. Meanwhile, your lower-middle family income bracket offered a modest boost toward success in the model's eyes, though your lower Year 1 decile of 3 (versus the average 5.71) added a smaller push toward the riskier side. Overall, while your standout test scores and GPA shine brighter than most, the model weighed your weaker school deciles—especially in Year 3—as the dominant red flags when stacked against typical students who maintain steadier rankings throughout. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law; it's the algorithm highlighting patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building a stronger late-stage academic record to lift those decile rankings, perhaps through targeted prep or additional credentials that address these gaps. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews that value your unique background more deeply. Keep pushing—your strengths position you well for another shot at success. We're rooting for you.
